Cookie Consent & Data Privacy

Site Documentation

Cookie Consent & Data Privacy

A full walkthrough of how cookie consent was implemented to comply with UK PECR and UK GDPR — including the legal reasoning, technical decisions, and why third party scripts require explicit user consent.

1

Overview

A cookie consent banner was added to this site to comply with UK data privacy law. The banner appears on a visitor's first visit and gives them a clear choice to accept or decline non-essential cookies before any third party scripts are loaded.

The decision to implement this was driven by two things — legal compliance under UK PECR, and a recognition that employers visiting this portfolio would notice the presence or absence of proper data privacy handling.

2

UK Law — PECR & UK GDPR

Two pieces of legislation are relevant to cookies on UK websites. The Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations (PECR) specifically govern cookies and similar tracking technologies. UK GDPR (the UK's post-Brexit version of the EU's GDPR) governs how personal data is collected and processed more broadly.

Under PECR, websites must obtain informed consent before setting any non-essential cookies on a user's device. Essential cookies — those strictly necessary for the site to function — are exempt. Non-essential cookies, including those set by third party services like Google reCAPTCHA, require explicit opt-in consent before they are allowed to run.

The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) is the UK's data protection regulator and publishes guidance on what constitutes valid consent. Valid consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Pre-ticked boxes or implied consent do not meet this standard.

Essential vs Non-Essential Cookies 

Essential (no consent required):
  — Session cookies needed for core functionality
  — Security cookies
  — Load balancing cookies

Non-Essential (consent required):
  — Analytics cookies (Google Analytics)
  — Advertising cookies
  — Third party scripts (Google reCAPTCHA)
  — Social media tracking pixels
3

Cookies Used on This Site

Django Session Cookie

Functional

Set by Django to track guest usage of AI tools across page loads. Without this cookie, the 3 free uses system would not work for unregistered visitors. This is borderline essential as it serves a core functional purpose on the site.

Google reCAPTCHA

Non-Essential

Set by Google when the reCAPTCHA script loads on the contact form. Google uses this data across their wider network for fraud detection and potentially advertising purposes. This is clearly non-essential and requires explicit consent before the script is allowed to load.

Cookie Consent Preference

Essential

Stored in localStorage (not a cookie) to remember whether the visitor has accepted or declined cookies. This does not require consent itself under ICO guidance as it is strictly necessary to remember the user's own privacy preference.

4

Technical Implementation

The consent system was built entirely in plain JavaScript without any third party cookie management library. This keeps the implementation transparent, lightweight, and easy to explain.

How the banner works

On first visit, localStorage is checked for a stored consent preference. If none exists, the banner appears after a 1.5 second delay giving the page time to load fully. The delay prevents the banner from being the very first thing a visitor sees.

When the user clicks Accept, the preference is saved to localStorage and the reCAPTCHA script is dynamically injected into the page. When they click Decline, the preference is saved and the reCAPTCHA widget is hidden.

On subsequent visits, the banner does not appear. If the stored preference is accepted, reCAPTCHA loads immediately. If declined, it remains hidden.

Dynamic reCAPTCHA Loading 

function loadRecaptcha() {
  if (!document.querySelector('script[src*="recaptcha"]')) {
    const script = document.createElement('script');
    script.src = 'https://www.google.com/recaptcha/api.js';
    script.async = true;
    script.defer = true;
    document.head.appendChild(script);
  }
}

Why localStorage instead of a cookie

Storing the consent preference in localStorage rather than a cookie was a deliberate decision. localStorage data never leaves the browser — it is never sent to any server and cannot be read by third parties. It is also exempt from the cookie consent requirement itself under ICO guidance, making it the correct tool for storing a privacy preference. Using a cookie to store cookie consent would be circular and potentially non-compliant.

Why reCAPTCHA is loaded dynamically

The reCAPTCHA script was removed from the HTML head section where it had originally been placed. If left in the head it would load automatically on every page visit before the user had any opportunity to consent. By removing it and only injecting it via JavaScript after the user clicks Accept, the site is fully compliant — Google's script only runs when the user has explicitly permitted it.

5

Key Concepts Learned

PECR and UK GDPR

Two separate pieces of UK legislation govern data privacy on websites. PECR specifically covers cookies and electronic communications. UK GDPR covers personal data processing more broadly. Both must be considered when handling user data on a UK website.

Essential vs Non-Essential Cookies

Not all cookies require consent. Essential cookies that are strictly necessary for the site to function are exempt. Non-essential cookies — particularly those set by third party services — require explicit opt-in consent before they are allowed to run.

Third Party Scripts and Consent

Any third party script that loads from an external server has the potential to set cookies and collect data. The site owner is responsible for ensuring these scripts only load after the user has consented, even though the site owner has no control over what the third party does with the data once their script runs.

Dynamic Script Loading

JavaScript can create and inject script tags into the page dynamically at any point after load. This technique is used here to delay the loading of the reCAPTCHA script until after the user has consented. It is a powerful pattern used across many real world applications for performance and compliance reasons.

localStorage vs Cookies

localStorage and cookies both store data in the browser but behave differently. Cookies are sent to the server on every request and can be read by third parties. localStorage stays in the browser, is never transmitted, and can only be read by the site that set it. For storing a consent preference, localStorage is the correct and compliant choice.

DOMContentLoaded

JavaScript that interacts with HTML elements must wait until the page has fully loaded before running. The DOMContentLoaded event fires when the browser has finished parsing the HTML. Wrapping JavaScript in this event prevents null errors caused by scripts trying to access elements that do not yet exist in the page.

6

Future Improvements

Granular Cookie Preferences

Allow users to accept or decline specific categories of cookies separately — for example accepting functional cookies but declining analytics. This is the approach used by larger sites and gives users more meaningful control.

Cookie Preference Centre

A dedicated page where users can review and change their cookie preferences at any time after their initial choice. Under UK GDPR users have the right to withdraw consent as easily as they gave it.

Privacy Policy Page

A full privacy policy page explaining in plain English what data is collected, how it is used, how long it is retained, and what rights users have under UK GDPR. This is technically required for any site that processes personal data.

Consent Expiry

Store a timestamp alongside the consent preference and re-show the banner after a set period — typically 12 months. ICO guidance suggests consent should be refreshed periodically rather than stored indefinitely.

</>
AS.
